meta.title: The Dependency Atlas of Artificial Intelligence
meta.scope_statement: A family-level dependency ontology spanning mathematical foundations, representations, learning paradigms, objectives, mechanisms, architectures, training, memory, reasoning, assurance, infrastructure, and end systems. It covers the major enduring branches and the specifically identified modern omissions, but it is not a catalog of every paper, named model, benchmark, dataset, implementation, or product.
meta.reading_instruction: Read upward in the default view: higher layers integrate concepts within or beneath their layer. Same-layer edges represent specialization or composition, so the map is a directed acyclic dependency graph rather than a strict stack.
meta.editorial_note: Definitions are concept-specific. Nodes marked graph-derived context use the validated dependency graph to generate their role summary, example uses, and baseline limitation note; curated nodes have those supporting fields manually authored. This is an ontology draft, not a peer-reviewed encyclopedia.
completeness_contract: Every in-scope node has a unique concept definition, a role summary, at least two examples or downstream uses, a limitation note, a maturity label, an explicit terminal declaration, and at least one reference pointer.
Every dependency edge has a relationship type, strength, and human-readable rationale.
The build must pass schema, missing-reference, missing-dependency, duplicate-definition, layer-direction, and cycle checks.
Named model instances such as GPT, BERT, Llama, Qwen, Claude, and Gemini remain examples of families unless they introduce a reusable dependency pattern.
Completeness means complete against this declared family-level scope, not exhaustive over all AI research.
known_exclusions: Individual commercial or open model releases and version histories
Every benchmark, dataset, framework, library, accelerator, and vendor product
Fine-grained subvariants that do not introduce a distinct dependency pattern
Claims of universal historical priority where research lineages are disputed
A guarantee that emerging terminology or research directions after the update date will remain stable

layers.title: Mathematical & Computational Foundations
layers.subtitle: The formal substrate: mathematics, computation, control, and information.

layers.title: Data & Representations
layers.subtitle: How observations, state, supervision, and knowledge are encoded.

layers.title: Learning Paradigms
layers.subtitle: The source of learning signal and the relationship between data, environment, and learner.

layers.title: Objectives & Statistical Inference
layers.subtitle: The criteria optimized during learning and the procedures used to infer hidden structure.

layers.title: Computational Primitives & Mechanisms
layers.subtitle: Reusable operations from which architectures are assembled.

layers.title: Model & Architecture Families
layers.subtitle: Trainable function families that combine representations, objectives, and mechanisms.

layers.title: Training, Adaptation & Compression
layers.subtitle: Pipelines that turn architectures and data into usable models.

layers.title: Memory, Retrieval & Knowledge
layers.subtitle: Ways to store, retrieve, update, and attribute information beyond a single forward pass.

layers.title: Reasoning, Planning & Agency
layers.subtitle: Deliberation, search, action selection, tools, and closed-loop behavior.

layers.title: Alignment, Safety & Evaluation
layers.subtitle: Measurement, interpretation, constraints, governance, and assurance.

layers.title: Infrastructure & Serving
layers.subtitle: Hardware and software systems that train, deploy, scale, and observe AI.

layers.title: Systems & Applications
layers.subtitle: End-to-end intelligent products and scientific or autonomous systems.

nodes.label: Formal methods, specifications & proof obligations

proof obligations for generated code
robustness specifications
limitations: A guarantee is only as sound as its specification, abstraction, solver, and environmental assumptions.

nodes.label: Empirical scaling laws
nodes.definition: Empirical scaling laws fit predictable relationships between loss or capability and model size, data, training compute, or inference compute.
nodes.why_it_matters: They make expensive model development partly forecastable and turn scaling into an explicit resource-allocation problem.
examples: parameter–data–compute curves
loss extrapolation
test-time compute curves
limitations: Fits can break across architectures, data regimes, objectives, or task metrics and are not universal physical laws.

nodes.label: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)

examples: rubric-scored task suites
held-out regression tests

limitations: A benchmark can overfit to public examples, miss deployment-specific harms, or reward a proxy that diverges from the intended behavior.

examples: pairwise response preference studies
expert rubric review
user-experience and harm-rating studies
limitations: Human ratings can be noisy, biased, expensive, and sensitive to rubric design, sampling, annotator expertise, and aggregation choices.

dependencies.rationale: Human evaluation uses statistics and experimental design to sample tasks, compare systems, and aggregate judgments.

nodes.label: LLM-as-judge & automated critique

nodes.why_it_matters: LLM-as-judge methods can scale rubric-based scoring and critique, but they still need calibration against human judgments and checks for bias or instability.

automated labeling followed by human calibration
limitations: Judge models can inherit model biases, favor familiar style over correctness, and disagree with humans when prompts, rubrics, or domains shift.

nodes.why_it_matters: It can support origin claims, synthetic-data filtering, and forensic analysis without trusting a generic AI-content classifier.
examples: green-list token watermarks
watermarked code generation
text provenance signals
limitations: Paraphrasing, editing, translation, collusion, and distribution shift can weaken detection or create false accusations.

nodes.label: Formal verification of models

nodes.label: Verified and assurance-gated agents
nodes.definition: Verified agentic systems place formal checks, typed actions, runtime monitors, permissions, and release gates around agents that can modify software or external state.
nodes.why_it_matters: They define a deployable pattern for giving agents consequential authority without treating generated actions as trusted by default.
examples: proof-gated coding agents
policy-checked enterprise automation
safety-shielded robots
limitations: Assurance coverage remains partial when goals, tools, observations, and environments are open-ended.
